speaker: interviewer
text: On January 9, 1996. And you were telling us about being in Abu Kabir with Elsa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the absorption center?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, what I'll tell you now. We have been two years in the, in the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And then while we been there, we applied for [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That was built in Bat Yam. When we went there to see where it will be built, we, we went until our knees in sand. And later was a settlement for 20,000 people, and beautiful. And we moved in into a apartment. There were houses of four apartment-- one-bedroom apartments. There were two-- part in the-- on the first floor and one on the, on the, on the other floor. And we had a, an apartment with a balcony on the, on the first floor. And then we started to work. We got in touch with, with bureau of art. And he said-- from the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] groups of [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] and we started to work in, in the kibbutzim. And Elsa went everywhere with us. And we started to work. There was one kibbutz of Hakim-- it was not far-- where they had a, a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] This was their main source of money, that the people came there to rest. And we gave counsel there. And we sang in Yiddish and all kind of languages. But being in the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], I had then a cousin who was in the military of [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], a first cousin. And he used to come every Saturday to visit us. He knew very well Yiddish and Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he studied with me the Hebrew songs. And I studied quite a few Hebrew songs that I sang in, in our concerts. There was very popular-- I don't know if you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a song, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. When, when-- it was the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], when Jerusalem was cut off from Tel Aviv. And the main [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] was cut off. So they wrote a song, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] And my brother-in-law, my husband's brother, Shmu Fershko-- maybe you heard of him. He was connected with the Temple Emmanuel. He was musical director there. He wrote the music while he was in, in Israel. And, and it was very, very popular. And [INAUDIBLE] recorded it. And it was sang every day in the radio. And so while being in the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], I met there a wonderful friend, Gavriil Waisman. He was a writer, a poet. And I told him, I said, Gavriil, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is so popular. Translate it into Yiddish. And he knew how. And he made a beautiful translation. And I started to sing it in Yiddish. I will never forget the first appearance in, in Israel. It was in Tel Aviv, in, in, on [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] It was from [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] And they were arranged. And we were invited to give a, a program. It was the first time that I sang in Hebrew. And, and when I sang, they came over. And they started to speak to me in Hebrew. [HEBREW SPEECH]. And I looked at them. I didn't know what to answer. And I said, please talk to me Russian, Polish, or Yiddish. I don't understand Hebrew. They couldn't believe it. I told them, I learned phonetically the Hebrew language. And every word that I sang in my song, I understand it because I have got to interpret the song. And, and that was the first appearance. And from then, we went to the kibbutzim. Because they got acquainted with our work. And we worked in the kibbutzim until there came a delegation from America. From all the cities, the heads of the Histadrut [? can  pay. ?] And there was a very big gathering in, in Tel Aviv. And in a big hall was a, a great banquet. And [? Shprintze ?] was the speaker. And we gave a program for the first time. It was, for them, the first time, and for us, the first, first time. And they couldn't get over it. Because they heard Yiddish songs that they didn't hear, and, and whatever I sang. And they got in touch with us. And they said-- through the Histadrut, in, in, in Israel-- that such a couple could bring more use for Israel and be in the States than being in Israel. And they started to work on it. It took two years until, until everything-- they could, they could take us there. Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a change. In America came-- Eisenhower came with [INAUDIBLE]. And McCarthy was then. And we were from Poland. So we thought that we will, we will be contrabanded to, to, to America from Poland. OK. And so they ask us to give a concert in Israel. We gave, in Tel Aviv, a concert. And the whole-- yes, and Elsa was supposed to go with us. So my husband was an accompanist, a pianist. And she was supposed to be a companion.

speaker: interviewer
text: They said, why should they give? And they gave-- it's the truth-- told them that we are invalids. And we need somebody to-- so they hesitated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came, all, to the concert. After the concert, the next day, the Histadrut got a phone call that all three of us got the visas, and that we can set a date when to come. And we left in August, '54, to take part in the beginning of the season of '55. And that's how we came to the States. And we started to work for Histadrut. We took part in the campaign and went all over the United States and Canada with Isaac Hamlin, who was a marvelous speaker. And he spoke in Yiddish. And there were thousands of people who did understand. And this was in 1955, 1950s. In 1959 came [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a, a friend, a colleague of my husband, who didn't know that he's alive, who didn't know. But he came from Los Angeles, with the play, And he was the first violinist of the orchestra. So Styne, Jule Styne, was the producer of the play, with Ethel Merman.

speaker: interviewer
text: He brought the concert maestro with him. And he had a hobby to look over names in telephone books. And he found a Fershko, a Chaim Fershko. He said, Chaim? So he-- and in Poland, they called him Edmund. He said, maybe it's the Fershko-- I went to school with a Fershko. He called up. And it was that Fershko.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came to our hotel where we used to live then, in a hotel. And he got in touch with us. And he saw what happened to us. He heard my husband play. He said, and you haven't been at So you know, to come to you have to have a pull. So he was the pull.

speaker: interviewer
text: He brought us to in 1959. I showed you the book. And we sang a Hebrew song. We sang-- what is the name? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a really-- they liked it very much. And I have to tell you that was a marvelous human being. He was surrounded with people that didn't let come closer to him. But while we were there-- and he appreciated very much, Elsa. When he heard the story that she's with us, and we were really dressed. And when we were with her, we didn't feel that we are handicapped. And I wore my prosthesis. And so he was really wonderful. And we appeared. And then all the doors were opened. It was the first time that we went to Mexico. And, and they heard we've been on All the doors were opened. And we started to-- and when we came back, we have got to go through a manager. So we were introduced to a manager. And when we came back from Mexico, I came to this manager. And he said, you come back, come to see me. I met his secretary. She said, he was a young fellow, 41 years old. He went on the bus and dropped dead, a heart attack. And that's was the end of Because the other one didn't know us and didn't have the same pull. And that's it. So we were once. But we had a lot of, of mail that we got. And we were introduced by what-- the journalist that he wrote in in New York, whatever it was-- Wilson, Wilson, a very well-known journalist. We were introduced. And he wrote that the station got a lot of mail about Sara and Chaim Fershko, about their appearance. And then we went on. And after we finished with, with the contract of Histadrut, we started to work. And we started to arrange concerts and work for all kind of Jewish organizations. And we, we made bigger and bigger, our repertoire. I started to sing, also, beautiful songs in English. And we, we were very successful in town hall. We had, every year, a concert in town hall, in New York. And then we, we started to come to Florida. And we used to have here annual concerts, besides the organizations. And we had a following until 1982. It was our last concert here when my husband-- nobody saw it. Nobody talked about it. But I felt it. He was supposed to give me an introduction for a song. And he gave me the introduction for another song. But me being musically, I, I found the tone for the other song that I have got to sing. And I started to sing the other song. And he went after me. And then the concert was wonderful until the end, even his solo. When he came home, I said to Elsa, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Elsa, Chaim made such a mistake that we couldn't-- he knew all the repertoire by heart. He didn't, didn't, didn't need the music. We went to doctor. And they started to look what's going on. And they found that he starts with Alzheimer's. And it starts in one spot on the brain. So then we retired. And in 1955, he passed away. It was not from the Alzheimer's. But the Alzheimer's, I was afraid he'll become a vegetable. I wouldn't give him anywhere. I was looking after him. But he, he started to have some heart trouble. And that's what. And, and Elsa passed away before, it-- almost in a year. So I have tomorrow, it's the yahrzeit of my husband. And tonight, I'll have to, to kindle a candle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But to tell you the story, when we married in 1949, I became pregnant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very early-- I was maybe three weeks or-- I took my darling husband, and I said, look, Chaim. We don't know what will be with us. We don't know what life has prepared for us. We are both handicapped people. We didn't know yet, Elsa. And I know you lost a child. I would like to have a child, too. But circumstances do not allow. I aborted my first child. And I never had any more. And in a way, I am glad. I didn't want to bring a child in a poisoned world. And I feel sorry for every baby that get born. And I pray to Go that they will grow up and live in the better world that we are now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, is there a message you would like to leave for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to leave a message for future generations that the Holocaust shouldn't be forgotten. And six million shouldn't be only mentions at the number. Because there were six million human beings with their own ideas, with their own lives. And those survivors of the numbers who remained were spread all over the world. And they have got to restore their life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They have got to restore their identity, their humanity, their beliefs, and their names. And they tried to make a life for themselves. And they are all heroes, wherever they went, wherever they were spread. They have got to learn a new language and to be-- and to join a new life in a new country. And they brought, also, a lot of culture wherever they went. We have a lot of survivors who, who became scholars, who became well-known in the, in the business world. And in any field of knowledge, our survivors took part.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the all generations should be taught that such a monstrosity shouldn't, shouldn't happen anymore like the Holocaust, and that the survivors taught them a lesson that humanly, we can find the human and mutual language, always, whatever we will be. And we shouldn't fight. We should discuss.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a very good saying that great minds discuss ideas. Middle minds discuss events. And small minds discuss people. And our world became very, very small. Because violence rules the world. And I hope that the future generations will bring up the world, that it will be led by the ideals of the great minds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your testimony with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want me to recite--

speaker: interviewer
text: Speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: We talked all about everything. But here, in Miami Beach, we have a memorial for the-- from the Holocaust. And it took quite a while to build it. And there were also people who didn't want to build it. And they wrote a [INAUDIBLE] letter into And then because the-- it was a sculpture of an outstretched hand. And it was too much for them to see an outstretched hand with figures who, who strived to remain alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a meeting in the city hall with all the commissioners and with the then-mayor, Daoud. And both sides were discussing their dispute. I was asked to say a few words.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I started to talk, I said that how ironic it is that I lost an, an arm in, in the Holocaust. And my, my lost arm is maybe the arm that is outstretched for life for others. My late husband lost a arm in the Holocaust. He was a great musician. Luckily, he played with one hand as others with two. And this lost arm said that more such monstrosities shouldn't take place. And I reminded myself that in my repertoire, I had a wonderful song called 'Never Forget.' But after my husband's death, I lost my voice. And for nine years, I couldn't bring out a word. But I will recite this song now. 'From six million nameless graves, we rise in gray, endless waves. We come to be near you, to haunt you, to hear you cry, peace! Shalom! From ovens of death, we'll rise, our shadows, across the skies. The young ones, the old ones, the weak ones, the bold ones, cry peace! Shalom! To us, you once bore an oath. Never forget, nor forgive! We'll rise from our endless sleep to judge you who live. The beaten beast grows again, grows fresh, fiery breath. All mankind now trembles, assembles its learning for death, for death. From six million nameless graves, we'll rise in gray, endless waves. We come to be near you, to haunt you, to hear you cry, peace! Shalom! Peace! Shalom! Peace! Peace! Shalom!' [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After that, Mayor Daoud cried. And he said the memorial will be built as it is. And you have a review I gave you, the paper that was written about it. And thank God that I took part in it, to help to build it. And now, I want to recite a Yiddish song. [YIDDISH SPEECH] The Jewish people are life. [YIDDISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. I'm ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you make it a little bit-- my mother a little bit?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll zoom into that [INAUDIBLE]. When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dear, I don't know. It was taken in Lutsk. It's from a book of the, the shul. It was from a group taken. And I made-- I enlarged it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names? Sara, their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Rysia Spitzman. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] It was found after the war in somebody's-- somebody had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my father. This picture was taken in the group from a [? boat ?] of the, of the shul. We had a shul 300 years old in Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mosze Spitzman.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the first commission in Lutsk, from the city hall of Lutsk. This was in 1931. And my father was a commissioner then. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah, this. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of the symphonic choir, the Ukrainian symphonic choir of Lutsk-- the conductor, Leon Weiner. It's before-- two days before we left for Kiev. This is the whole ensemble. Now here, this is my husband, still with both hands. And here I am, still with both hands. And this was taken in 1941, in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Chaim Fershko's first wife, Dora Dobkowska, a great opera singer, who gave the first concert in Warsaw Philharmonic in 1932. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is their son, five-year-old Moishele Fershko. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were both killed by the Nazis in cold blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture from [INAUDIBLE]. I played [? Leia. ?] Those are my own braids. And those, the other ones-- will you show the other ones, too? Those are scenes from the play, when [? Hunan ?] meets, meets [? Leia, ?] and the meshulach is in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year? What year was this done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was in, in 1946, '47. This is the whole group of the theatre and well-known Jewish writers. [? Yonasovich ?] is here. And I don't remember now the names. They came from Lutsk to the premier of the [INAUDIBLE]. Ensemble of a concert that we gave in [INAUDIBLE] in 1948, for the Haganah, after the proclamation of Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm here on this picture. Here am I, and the great Polish actress, Maia Malicka, and [? Dolgowa, ?] a violinist, and the director of the Polish Theatre. And here is my husband, who organized and played. And this is Gavriil Waisman, who wrote the song, 'Haganah,' the words to the song, 'Haganah,' that we sang there. It was the motto of the concert.

speaker: interviewer
